text: Laparoscopic repairs supplemented with perineal approach for perineal hernia after abdominal perineal resection are video big net. This is a case of an eighty-year-old woman with low BMI who underwent laparoscopic APR without the resection of extra-arabeta muscles for lower rectal cancer. The pathological diagnosis was stage IIIc The patient received adjuvant chemotherapy. One year later, she underwent hysterectomy for pyometola. Three years after the resection, the patient complained of perineal bulge and effusion. The CT scan shows protrusion of the small ball through the pelvic floor into the perineal region. A perineal hernia was diagnosed.

The laparoscopic view shows the dissection of severe adhesions between the intestine and pelvic wall using monopolar electrocautery and scissors. The surgeon lifts the vagina by suturing to secure a better view of the pelvis, taking care not to damage the small intestine.

The surgeon moves the intestine cranially to expose the hernia orifice, measuring seven centimeters by seven centimeters. A three-centimeter margin is marked circumferentially around the orifice. The ventral right ST mesh is shaped and inserted into the abdomen through a port in the right lower quadrant. The mesh is fixed laterally and posteriorly using absorbable tacks, while the patency of the iliac arteries and veins is visually confirmed.
The surgeon sutures the mesh at the anterolateral side to prevent damage to the ureter, then expands the mesh to cover the pelvic floor. The laparoscopic procedure is completed.

The surgeon demonstrates the mesh fixation method using a lightweight monofilament polypropylene mesh, trimmed to a three-centimeter circumferential margin. The anterior side of the mesh is fixed to the posterior wall of the vagina, the posterior side to the regenerated membrane, and the lateral side to the pelvic floor muscles. Absorbable tacks are used postero-laterally, and absorbable sutures are used antero-laterally. A skin incision is made at the site of the perineal hernia, and the herniated sac is opened. Artisanal sutures are placed around the perineal side of the mesh under direct vision, and a drain is inserted through the perineal side under the mesh. The perineal sac and skin are trimmed and closed.

The total operative time was two hundred forty-six minutes and blood loss was two hundred cc. There were no post-operative complications. As a four-month follow-up, there was no recurrence at the perineal site. In conclusion, this technique is feasible and safe and may provide promising surgical options for the repair of post-operative perineal hernias.
